1: hallĕlû ʿabdê yhwh hallĕlû ʾet-šēm yhwh — the triple repetition of hallĕlû opens Ps 113 with liturgical intensity; this is the first of the Egyptian Hallel psalms (113–118) sung at Passover. The address is ʿabdê yhwh (servants of the LORD) — corporate Israel understood as YHWH's household. šēm yhwh (the name of the LORD) — to praise the name is to praise the character and presence as revealed, not merely a label. This distinction matters: the nations will eventually praise this name (Ps 117; Romans 15:11).
2: yĕhî šēm yhwh mĕbōrāk mēʿattāh wĕʿad-ʿôlām — "may the name of the LORD be blessed from now and until forever" — the jussive yĕhî (let it be) frames this as prayer, not merely description. The temporal span mēʿattāh wĕʿad-ʿôlām (from this moment and unto eternity) appears also in Psalms 115:18 and Psalms 121:8, making it a standard formula for eschatological praise.
3: mimmizraḥ-šemeš ʿad-mĕbōʾô mĕhullāl šēm yhwh — "from the rising of the sun to its setting, praised is the name of the LORD" — spatial universality (east to west) complements the temporal universality of v2. The passive participle mĕhullāl (praised, being praised) suggests an already-existing reality: the name is universally worthy of praise as a present fact. Malachi 1:11 echoes this: YHWH declares that his name is great among the nations from east to west.
4: rām ʿal-kol-gôyim yhwh ʿal-haššāmayim kĕbôdô — "the LORD is high above all nations; above the heavens is his glory" — rām (Qal participle of rûm, to be high) is simple in Hebrew but sweeping in claim: YHWH stands above every political and religious sovereignty. Even the heavens, the highest created reality, are below his glory. The transcendence established here (vv4-5) makes the condescension of vv6-9 all the more radical.
5: mî kayhwāh ʾĕlōhênû haggābihî lāšābet — "who is like the LORD our God, who is enthroned so high (haggābihî lāšābet)?" — the rhetorical question expects no answer. haggābihî is the Hiphil participle of gābah (to be high, to raise high): he who makes his seat high. The idiom "high to sit/dwell" emphasizes his enthronement at the apex of creation — precisely the one who then bends down in vv6-9.
6: hammašpîlî lirʾôt baššāmayim ûbāʾāreṣ — "who lowers himself to look at the heavens and the earth" — hammašpîlî (Hiphil participle of šāpal, to be low, humble) — God must lower himself to look even at the heavens. The condescension is absolute: the one enthroned above heaven bends down so far that even the sky is below his sight-line. This framing makes vv7-9 theologically radical: his attention to the poor is an act of infinite descent.
7: mĕqîmî mēʿāpār dāl mēʾašpōt yārîm ʾebyôn — "raising the poor from the dust, lifting the needy from the ash heap" — the vocabulary is nearly identical to 1 Samuel 2:8 (Hannah's song). The ʾašpōt (ash heap, dunghill) in ancient villages was the rubbish heap outside the village where the destitute scavenged — a specific social location, not a metaphor. Ps 113 deliberately evokes Hannah's song, connecting YHWH's saving action with the OT pattern of reversal (Sarah, Rebekah, Rachel, Hannah, Elizabeth). Luke 1:52-53 (the Magnificat) continues this pattern.
8: lĕhôšîbî ʿim-nĕdîbîm ʿim nĕdîbê ʿammô — "to seat him with princes, with the princes of his people" — nādîb (prince, noble, willing giver) has dual meaning: nobility by birth and liberality of spirit. The God who lifts the poor from the ash heap seats them in the company of the nobility. This is not social revolution through violence but divine reversal through grace — the same pattern appears in the Magnificat and in Jesus' beatitudes.
9: mōšîbî ʿăqeret habbayit ʾēm-habbānîm śĕmēḥāh — "who gives the barren one of the house a home, a joyful mother of children" — ʿăqeret (barren woman, from ʿāqar, to root up/hamstring) — one who has been uprooted from the social role of motherhood. Her transformation into ʾēm-habbānîm śĕmēḥāh (a joyful mother of children) closes the psalm on the note of reversal. The barren woman's transformation is the paradigm of all the divine reversals in the Psalter.
